reviews.review_text:
Some games are ones you delete after you finish them.

Some games are ones you remember even years later.

Resident Evil 4 falls into the second category for me.

When I first started playing, I thought I was just going to play a high-quality remake. But as the hours passed, I realized that Capcom hadn't just revamped an old game; they had brought back to life a legend that players have loved for years.

The atmosphere is incredible. From the moment you enter the village, you constantly feel pressure. You don't know what's around the next corner, you're not sure if you have enough ammunition, and you always feel like something is going to go wrong. That's what makes Resident Evil 4 special.

Leon is fantastic as always. His more mature and experienced self adds a new depth to the story. The side characters, boss battles, and unforgettable scenes have also been meticulously recreated.

The gameplay is addictively good. You think, "Let me play one more level," then you look at the clock and it's midnight. The combat is fluid, inventory management is still fun, and the knife mechanics add a whole new flavor to the game.

But what impressed me most was this:

This game reminded me why I love video games.

It gave me an experience where I didn't have to constantly follow quest markers, waste time on unnecessary things in an open world, and was simply immersed in a well-designed adventure.

Many games today strive to offer larger maps, more content, and longer gameplay.

Resident Evil 4, however, chooses to simply be fun.

And it does it almost perfectly.

If you've played this game before, it will remind you why it's a classic.

If you're playing it for the first time, you'll understand why everyone has been talking about Resident Evil 4 for years.

Some games are the best of their time.

Some transcend their time.

Resident Evil 4 is one of them.

✨ "The game was great. The memories it created were even better."

I don't like horror games. I never played a single resident evil game. I never had an interest in the original games. I gave it a shot anyways.

I finally get it now.
I get why this is some people's comfort game.




also leon hot

Resident Evil 4 is a masterpiece, but honestly this review is for the Merchant.

No idea why he’s there. No idea how he keeps getting there before me. No idea why he has a rocket launcher in his coat.

But every time I see that blue flame and hear “What are ya buyin’?” my blood pressure drops.
He doesn’t question anything. He just buys ♥♥♥♥, offers advice, sells guns, cheers you on at the shooting range, and somehow feels like the safest person in Spain.

10/10.
Capcom, please bring back the Merchant in a future RE game.
I’ll buy it at a high price.
The game jump scared me so bad that i pooped my pants, 10/10 Edit: I'm very sad now, all the towns people had a bingo night and I wasn't invited :( 9.5/10
playing through all of the resident evils for the first time and this is defently one of the better ones resident evil games as a whole is 100000/10

I really enjoyed playing this one, between original or remake I loved this one more. (Still do love both of course ♡) 
Now for the characters, never really hate to be honest. Also Leon and Ada, gotta love them. <3
The separate ways story it was also good, it was fun playing as Ada. and even seeing Albert Wesker aswell still love him.

10/10 very impressive remake.so i used to play original game like a 10 years ago at my playstation i would have to say its amazing.in2019 re2 was remake that point make me to waiting other episodes to remake. finally the waiting end with the most impressive remake of all time in my opinion. The gameplay very well and enjoyable , The story easy to understand and price is very valuable.

there is a few things in this game that make it grating, Artificial difficulty, horrible hit detection, leon has aim like he has parkinsons... the Del Lago fight is a coin flip when you throw harpoons. ill never understand why people give them a pass for this, its no wonder developers get away with being completely lazy. This is otherwise a fantastic remake, its like they resorted to stuff like this because they knew the game would be too easy otherwise and didnt want to put the time in to fixing and balancing it, which is on par with capcom, they release something like this they move on. :( at least most of this stuff is fixed in re9, other than the movement jank.
It's a great game. The graphics are amazing, I love the guns, fights, story etc
Still playable, even though i can play this game later for next spec pc

I originally bought Resident Evil 4 because everyone said it was one of the greatest games ever made.

After 50+ hours of gameplay, extensive research, and several medically unexplainable incidents, I can confidently say that everyone was wrong.

Resident Evil 4 is not a game.

It is an elaborate Albert Wesker viewing simulator with occasional interruptions from parasites, cultists, and some guy named Leon.

The gameplay? Idgaf.

The story? Idgaf.

The atmosphere? Idgaf.

The graphics? Idgaf.

Albert Wesker?

I genuinely lack the vocabulary to describe what happens to me when this man appears on screen.

Every single time I hear that voice, see those sunglasses, or watch him stand there with the confidence of someone who has never once experienced embarrassment, my soul leaves my body and files a restraining order against me.

The first time he appeared I paused the game.

The second time he appeared I stood up.

The third time he appeared I was pacing around my room explaining to nobody in particular why he was the greatest fictional character ever conceived by mankind.

My friends told me I was overreacting.

I showed them Wesker.

They understood.

Scientists should study this man.

How do you create a character that says approximately three sentences, appears for SEVEN minutes, and somehow steals the spotlight from literally everyone else?

Leon is fighting for his life.

Ashley is being kidnapped.

Entire villages are collapsing.

Meanwhile I'm sitting there staring at Wesker like a Victorian woman seeing an ankle for the first time.

At one point I had to pause my game because I physically could not focus on the objective after seeing him.

The game wanted me to continue progressing.

I wanted to continue staring.

These were conflicting goals.

Wesker walks into a scene like he personally owns gravity.

The sunglasses stay on.

The coat stays immaculate.

The aura remains unmatched.

Bae talks like every sentence costs $500,000.

Every line delivery sounds like he's moments away from either taking over the world or making me develop a completely new category of emotional problems.

And let's talk about the voice.

Who approved that?

Who heard that voice and thought, "Yes, let's release this to the public."

There should have been a warning label.

"CAUTION: Extended exposure to Albert Wesker may result in loss of concentration, emotional instability, and spontaneous screaming."

I am no longer capable of hearing that voice without staring into the distance for several minutes.

My family asks if I'm okay.

I tell them Wesker appeared.

They nod solemnly.

They've seen what happens.

By the end of the game I wasn't crying.

Crying is an understatement.

I became a waterfall.

Tears streamed down my face.

Down my neck.

Down my shirt.

Down my legs.

Down my thighs...

Entire hydroelectric power plants were constructed from the force of my sorrow whenever he left the screen.

Local governments classified me as a renewable water source.

Marine biologists discovered three previously unknown fish species living in the rivers created by my grief.

When the credits rolled, I sat in silence.

Not because I was reflecting on the incredible journey.

Not because I was appreciating one of gaming's greatest masterpieces.

But because I realized there would be no more Wesker appearances.

I have not fully recovered.

Resident Evil 4 changed my life.

Sometimes I wake up in the middle of the night and think about that man standing there in his stupid sunglasses looking cooler than every human being to ever exist.

Then I stare at the ceiling for three hours.

10/10.

Masterpiece.

Game of the Year.

Game of the Decade.

Game of the Century.

Would willingly become an Umbrella test subject if Albert Wesker asked politely.

10/10 simply amazing. This is by far a superior upgrade over the previous titles with both gameplay and story. The characters are always amazing, a standard well set by Resident Evil. Having both Leon and Ada in a game together is always interesting. The bosses are excellent here, there is a lot of variety. The action sequences satisfy your need without any doubt, so much different scenarios played out, it feels like more than one game with the impressive change of scenery between each location. The game length is impressive, 3 large areas each of them unique yet still relevant with the plot. Ashley is a great addition to your journey, adding a layer of difficulty and fun to the already great gameplay, the inventory management and the fighting mechanics are great. One of the best if not the best Resident Evil game there is.

I came for back flips, one liners and brum merchants. And I was not disappointed.
10/10 would recommend, have a good day Strangers.
Here after REQUEIM And still this game's replaybility is unmatched

Compared to other RE games, re4 is very much on the lighter side of horror. Re4 doesn't keep you on edge with the stalker/horror mechanics like re2, so if you're a scaredy cat like me it's a great way to start, since it's way more an action horror then a survival horror! Leon can do anything in this game. He can parry a chainsaw, dodge, backflip, kick people, backflip again and so much more. Plus we have the goated merchant. Though 50% of the time you will need to babysit Ashley, but we love Ashley so it's fine.  
Overall the game is a masterpiece. Enjoyed till the last minute. It's a great remake from the original, only missing some of Leon’s amazing one liners! This game will always have a dear place in my heart!<3

It’s very popular nowadays to compare the remake to the original 2005 game.
 
But why?

Both are great, both were made by Capcom, so what’s all the fuss about? Some people prefer the original, others prefer the remake - and that’s perfectly fine. 
Capcom reimagined and modernized a 20-year-old game - why not?

If you look at the remake on its own, it’s an amazing game! It’s fun to play, fun to shoot things, fun to explore stuff and collect treasures. 

This is a 10/10 game... no... 9/10. Because, for Christ’s sake, please make Ashley stop moaning all the time. Sometimes it feels like I’m not playing a survival horror game, but watching P0rnhub.

I don't write reviews but this game made me.

I came in fresh off RE2 Remake which blew me away with the RPD. That building is a masterpiece of game design and I went into RE4 expecting the same thing. It felt more linear and I noticed. Then I played Separate Ways and had to completely eat my words. By the end I genuinely think RE4 does it better than RE2, you just won't see it until the second playthrough.

In something like Left 4 Dead or The Last of Us the infected are basically just predators; brainless, hunting you, that's it. Scary in a fun way. The cultists in RE4 are something else entirely. These are just normal people: farmers, villagers, normal people, who got pulled into something and never came back. They're not hunting you because they're mindless, they're hunting you because they believe in something. That's way scarier and the game never lets you forget it.

Opening a door to the Merchant genuinely feels like running into a friend. You hear that music, see the lantern, and just exhale. Even when I avoided him on a challenge run, I still felt happy seeing him.

The combat just clicks in a way most games don't. Every fight is a little puzzle positioning, ammo, knowing when to knife and when to run. I liked it so much I ended up doing a couple of different playthroughs and at no point did it feel unfair.

Also Ashley is way better than people give her credit for and I will not be taking questions.

Just play it.

10/10
That's already the third time I complete the game and I don't get tired of it! It's my favourite of the series out of all those I played to so far.

All I have to say is good God Leon Kennedy. The transformation of his character development from RE2 to this version of him alone is worth buying it. The story is amazing, but for some reason my heart still goes back to the story in RE2. Compared to the first version released of RE4 the remake definitely exceeds expectations.
Nice game, good game, and i love ada wong.
Accidently ended up playing for 12 hours straight and beat the game for the first time. Absolute masterpiece.

TLDR: 
Most Versatile RE Game till date
Graphics - 10/10
Combat - 10/10
Story - 5/10
Main Villain - 1/10
Replayability - 10/10

This is the most hours I have invested in a Resident Evil Game. There is always one more sub-objective, one more treasure, one more hidden weapon, which keeps you coming back to it and never feels like a chore. The difficulty is manageable, mostly because you play as an overpowered character with an arsenal better than that of UN.

The ambience and level design is just top notch. Most systems can handle it well as it is very well optimized. Every gun feels different with a unique kick and sound, and makes you want to make custom runs with different setups.
While everything is just brilliant about the game, I did feel its story progression and presentation was inferior to RE4 (2005) and the antagonist has been demoted to a bland old man who gets bullied multiple times before getting stabbed in his over exposed eye in the end.

I would recommend this to anyone whose pc can run it. It is a perfect blend of adventure, action, survival and horror.

As a longtime fan of the Resident Evil series, playing Resident Evil 4 remake was such an amazing experience from start to finish. I’ve played other Resident Evil titles across different gaming platforms over the years, so jumping into this remake felt both familiar and completely fresh at the same time.

What I loved most about the game was the creepy atmosphere. The dark villages, eerie castles, disturbing enemy designs, and intense sound effects constantly kept me on edge. Even when I expected something scary to happen, the game still managed to surprise me with its jumpscares and tense moments. There were so many times where I caught myself saying “nope” while enemies came rushing toward me from every direction. Haha.

One thing the remake does perfectly is making you feel overwhelmed. Ammo feels limited, enemies never seem to stop coming, and every fight feels chaotic in the best possible way. Whether it was getting cornered by villagers or trying to survive intense encounters with powerful enemies, the pressure and panic made the experience incredibly fun and memorable.

I did run into some graphical and performance issues at first since I'm only using an rtx 30 series card, even though my pc has 32gb ram. Some areas would lag or stutter during intense scenes, especially when a lot was happening on screen. Thankfully after spending some time fine-tuning the graphics settings, I managed to get the game running smoothly without sacrificing too much visual quality. Once everything was optimized, the experience became much more enjoyable.

The combat feels smoother and more modern while still keeping the identity of the original game. I also really enjoyed how the remake expanded certain moments and characters while staying faithful to what made the original so iconic. It honestly reminded me why I became such a huge Resident Evil fan in the first place.

For me, this wasn't just a remake — it was a nostalgic return to one of the best survival horror experiences ever made. The game balances horror, action, tension, and nostalgia so well that I ended up enjoying every minute of it. Definitely one of my favorite Resident Evil experiences so far.

Resident Evil 4 is easily one of the best remakes ever made. The game perfectly preserves the soul of the original while modernizing every aspect of the gameplay, visuals, and atmosphere. Combat feels incredibly satisfying, the pacing is excellent from beginning to end, and every encounter keeps you tense and engaged.

The graphics are stunning, with detailed environments, amazing lighting, and cinematic presentation that make every location memorable. Leon feels smoother and more responsive to control, while the enemy AI makes fights much more intense and strategic. The sound design is also phenomenal — from the eerie ambient noises to the chaotic battles against Granados, everything adds to the horror experience.

What impressed me most is how the remake respects longtime fans while still feeling fresh for new players. The game balances action and horror almost perfectly. Boss fights are exciting, exploration is rewarding, and the weapon upgrade system makes progression addictive.

Capcom truly put passion into this remake. Whether you played the original or not, this is a masterpiece that deserves every bit of praise it gets. Absolutely worth the price and one of the best survival horror games available on Steam. 10/10.
Ada wong threw that ass back so hard, I recommend you play this game and make sure to make her ass EVEN BIGGER than what it was before

Jogo muito bom!
Claramente aqui a franquia começa a buscar uma outra abordagem, o que me faz não ter esse game como o meu favorito da saga apesar de ter sim uma certa ligação nostálgica com o mesmo. O trabalho de remake é absurdo! Melhoraram demais o game que já era bom, em minha opinião, e com a história sendo muito boa isso tudo junto o torna indispensável. ENJOY!!

What is there not to like about re4r. The blend of both horror and action makes this game work so well that it transcends horror as a genre. While I did play re4r on my ps5 few months back, this game is more intended on the pc because of how much it immerses you in the resident evil universe.

Hated the original cause of the tank controls and dropped not even half way through..
So glad they remade it into this. I absolutely get it now why re4 is adored by many and now I am the one adoring it too. It is an incredible entry of a very strong franchise.

On hardcore, standard and assisted, I think this an exceptional action game. The strength of the original pacing is still present, and while I wouldn't say it's better than the original (the sidequests add bloat more than anything, the story takes itself more seriously, which in turn leads to a more boring series of cutscenes and character interactions, and I simply prefer the gunplay and movement of the OG), it is still a supremely enjoyable experience.

On professional however, I think the fun-factor kind of falls apart. Your melee abilities – which I would call a cornerstone of what makes RE4's combat loop unique – are neutered. I'm specifically talking about enemy staggers, and parrying. In order to land a parry, it has to be a so-called perfect parry, meaning you have to hit an incredibly tight window of about 6-12 frames. Miss it, and you're eating the full damage. This is about how difficult it is to land a parry in dark souls, and I just don't think it makes sense to do this for such a central mechanic, which you're constantly making use of on assisted to hardcore, in close to every encounter. On the easier difficulties, you have around a half-second grace period where the parry will land, which is generous. It still requires timing, but it's very doable. This incentivises you to not just stay back and pick enemies off: get in close, land a parry and/or or a stagger, and clean up with a roundhouse. On pro, where you additionally don't have any autosaves, I instead adopted the most boring playstyle imaginable, staying back and taking potshots to avoid close contact with the enemies whenever possible. I just didn't want to deal with a 30-minute long section all over again because of one missed, incredibly tight parry, that led to a subsequent stunlock and death. Could you call this a skill issue? Sure. If I could consistently land frame-perfect parries on every enemy type, I wouldn't be writing this. But you could also argue that this disincentivises close-quarters combat (in RESIDENT EVIL 4), and pushes you towards a passive and, in my opinion, unfun playstyle.

The staggers, or lack thereof, add to the push towards passivity. On pro, a large number of enemies will simply not stagger before they're killed. 3-4 shots to the head with the pistol? A burst with the TMP? Nothing, not a flinch. Aside from being supremely unsatisfying, I really question this design choice, because doesn't it just push you towards playing the game in a more boring, passive way? Like any other run-of-the-mill third person shooter? 

Still a great game though, and all of this can simply be avoided through other difficulties or bonus items.
Amazing game! However. Capcom, WHY DO I NEED TO PLAY THE DLC TO GET THE 100%, CAN'T YOU MAKE THE SECOND 100% ON THE SEPERATE WAYS?! but. overall, amazing game. Leon is kick ass.
I play it cracked a long time ago. I decided to buy it on sales to support the developer.
I didn't play the original game, but I will say that Resident Evil 4 Remake is a work of art and truly a masterpiece .

This guide is for anyone wanting to download and try Resident Evil 4 for the first time. First, the game has an epic storyline because you'll play as a character fighting monsters. I don't want to spoil the story for anyone, but trust me, you won't regret playing Resident Evil. Second, there's the additional content that comes out whenever you complete a chapter, and you'll be rewarded with coins or sometimes cool outfits for Leon Kennedy. The coins can be used to buy cosmetic items or clothes. Finally, I recommend everyone play Resident Evil, and believe me, you'll never regret playing this game.
Great Game good story and gameplay Graphics Are Great

Almost beat this game. It's truly an experience to say the least.
From the parrying, to the updated controls...
This game is overall way easier than the original, and that's not a bad thing.
The original was hard, that's a fact. This game changed that.
Most bosses (e: Del Lago) aren't such a pain in the ass than before.
Great remake, would recommend.

(P.S: There are a few problems with fatal D3D errors. My problem was that my hard drive bottleneck, due to me using an EXT-was causing the game to crash due to the demand not meeting transfer. That was fixed with a simple SS port.)
If you are looking for Skill = dominance and a fun game which is newgame+, this is the one for you, puzzles are good, not hard,

These constant Fatal d3d errors are quite annoying. If you're lucky to avoid those I guess the game itself is okay (except for the stuff that follows).

I definitely dislike the 'over-the-shoulder' design though as it feels like my character is constantly drunk. Then again my first entry into this franchise was RE7:Village (which was quite enjoyable). It's also very annoying that you're constantly 'counting bullets' because the game gives you very little while aiming can be quite a chore with the crosshair moving away from the target. However, I assume that's just the defective auto-aim 'feature' which only seems to lock on where the enemy was and then stays there regardless of the enemy's movement.

Lastly, and what I find most annoying, is the game's constant need to put you in these 'running fights' in close quarters where you're constantly swarmed by an endless stream of enemies. This results in you always running out of your dwindling ammo supplies only for some boss showing up when all you got is a half-broken knife. That last bit really takes the fun out of the game and turns it into a very frustrating experience where it's more luck than skill that wins the fight.

So the controls and over-the-shoulder view makes things very clunky while the endless zerg-mob 'encounters' are just a pain; but the environment, story, and little puzzles seem to make up for it somewhat.

However, Capcom really should have toned down the 'zerg zerg zerg!' crap because it just turns what could have been an enjoyable horror adventure into an endless slog of frustration.

Get it when its heavily discounted as (imo) it's not worth paying full price for. The main annoyances are requiring luck to get it even starting without those 'fatal d3d' bugs as well as the zerging enemies when you're forced into cramped spaces that are just there to drain what little ammo you were given.

I recently bought this on pc but I have over 100 hours on this game on ps5 this is one of the best resident evils you can play it has infinite replayability and the characters are great would recommend to anyone

From the moment I entered the village and the overwhelming chaos erupted from all sides, until the bell finally rang. I knew this was something special worth remembering. Epic, awesome, amazing.... best introduction ever—and it has only got better.

Obviously, yes... the game is huge. I remember playing the OG many years ago and the controlls were super ugly... 
This one has really good controlls. It's still hard at times but at least it depend on your skill of learning and experimenting. You can easily die and prioritize position and strategy the next time. As a treasure hunter, 
I got around 7h in and didn't even finish chapter 4.
The zone doesnt let me go back to a place tho... but I guess it will in late game.
Overall. Good story, graphics, well optimized. Peak game. One of the best for sure. This is how u make a remake. Tho, I rly love the RE2 and 3 as well. Although some people are criticising them.

One of the best games I've ever played, great mix of horror and action at the same time.

Ashley is my favorite character, she doesn't really do anything, she is just there to be all weak and sexy and vulnerable and all she does is open stuff and yells when she is attacked, most realistic portrayal of women, 10/10

This game truly is the peak gaming experience though, you have like 10 bullets, but you need to take out 20 zombies, good luck lol

And yes, all that money on the rocket launcher is worth it, it instantly clears a stage in a boss fight.
Very true to the original with some improvement on some characters like Luis which is great. Overall absolute blast to play Leon is so back and I'm all for it.

when i got into playing re i thought that this game would be so boring because its in some random village setting but i decided to buy it on sale i realized how peak it was and if u need any convincing to buy this game there is a guy called del lago.so tuff boiiii

The story is great visuals are even better. Overall, amazing.

Barely played the OG RE4 back in the PS2 days when I was 12 because I was so scared to play it. Years later, now I have the chance to get myself into it again with the REmake. A game worth being afraid of in my opinion.

P.S Leon is both a freaking badass and an idiot. Love him hahahaha
Obviously its amazing, its not called a masterpiece for nothin, got it on sale so feel even better i get to enjoy this masterpiece for the low low price. GET IT NOW!!

If you're expecting a game that is a replacement of the original, you've come to the wrong place. If you're thinking that this REmake is inferior to the original, that's also untrue, but there is some disagreements on where and what are not equal. This game however is very much a worthwhile investment of your time being an engaging survival horror title that is actually pretty equal to its original. I'm also not going to hold back from spoilers, so this is your warning if you care about stuff like that.

The Pros: The things I like about this version of RE4
- Your knife: As seen in the trailer, you can now parry oncoming enemy attacks with a knife. And while it does use up durability and you won't always be able to repair the knife, you can upgrade its durability to make it last longer between struggle sessions, as well as inferior disposable knives to have something if your main buddy is down for the count. Which is important because the game also enables Leon to be able to stealth kill, which is one of the two knife gameplay things in the RE4R that he couldn't do in the OG (along with the parry), and a life-saver in the war of attrition that is resource management.
- Tension: This one is made of a lot of smaller things, but the main thing is that this game is not so lenient as to allow one to beat it blindfolded (though I'd imagine someone probably has by this point). However, with smart resource management, you too can send enemies packing while being in despair over that one bullet that you're never getting back after it gets blocked by the environment. Even on Standard and Hardcore difficulties, this game will allow you to do what you want, but it's also not going to hold back from deploying the whipping squad to capitalize on your mistakes, so keeping your head on a swivel is very much recommended since the Ganados are far more assertive of their rights to crush Leon's windpipe in comparison to how they are in the OG game.
- The guns: If gun diversity is the quota you wanted, this game gives a healthy amount (15 guns + the rocket launcher and the bolt thrower + 2 main knives) the player can get from their natural playthrough and another 4 unlockable weapons from doing game clears on certain difficulties and one collectible achievement. In comparison, the OG gives 14 weapons + 4 unlockables (5 on every RE4 version after the Gamecube version), while there are weapons that are going to be off-meta in terms of how often players will use them, they are all useful in surviving the long night. 
-- Killer7: An extra note for the guns, but what started as a reference to Suda51 game Killer7 got redeemed in this version of the game for having two notable upgrades - a laser sight by default, and an exclusive that gives it an enhance crit chance (vs the OG that never gave it an exclusive upgrade).
- Ashley (characterization): Your damsel in distress has a nice character growth over the course of the game vs how she was in the original. While both versions have her as a plucky girl after Leon comes in on a white horse to escort her out of danger, RE4R's Ashley shows more complexity as someone who doesn't want to be the burden all the time and tries to help Leon out in small ways where she can. She won't be taking people to Suplex City any time soon, but she at least develops into someone who has more spine than a doormat.

The Maybe: Aka the parts of the game that are of questionable quality to the original
- Wince: This is a mechanic that has been in the RE Engine games that determines how likely an enemy is going to stagger. On normal difficulty, the player is most likely not going to notice this as they go for every headshot or legshot before running for the hills or setting up a collateral with kicks. But because of the game's adaptive difficulty, on Hardcore and especially on Professional difficulty, it will take far more shots to the head from your starting pistol to get someone to clench their head in pain or drop to their knees before firepower upgrades come into play.
- Melee attacks: On top of the wince mechanic, melee attacks are not as good in this game as they were in the OG. They still do fine damage, but as this is a remake of an already action focused survival horror game, the enemies tend to recover quickly from being staggered, and the game being slightly bigger than the OG in relative scale means that it takes more time to follow-up on your momentarily vulnerable victims, which means there are times when you can't use action prompts like the legendary German Suplex of the OG RE4, which many people used to save ammo and prevent Plagas from sprouting. Anecdotally, I was mostly using my gun, and i rarely ever actually used a Kick prompt. This is definitely a skill issue, but with the higher aggression of the enemies, it's often a better strategy to take them out from an entire zip code away if the player can help it and not risk the danger of exposing Leon's (relatively) fragile body to everything trying to turn him into a stump, especially since RE4R doesn't give i-frames for melee attacks.
- Ashley (gameplay): In the OG RE4, Ashley has three modes. They are: Stand here, follow me (at the hip), and "do this thing" on certain interactable objects in the game. And while there are still interactable things in RE4R for Ashley to do things with like hide in lockers for safety, her other options are now "stay at my hip" and "give me room to swing a bat." There are times when the game will have Ashley enforce Leon's social distancing rules to not get any closer, but having Ashley stay further back than at the hip opens her up to being swept off her feet by a flirty dinner date that she never asked for, and a more likely chance of starting over at the last checkpoint from Ashley being stolen while Leon is trying to flip kick a way out of the hell hole they're trapped in.
- Ada's EN voice-over: It's not a new or uncommon opinion, but Lily Gao's delivery of Ada's English lines sound like someone asleep at the wheel or reading lines while flipping through a magazine. While I could blame her EN VA for sounding not up to par to Ada's other voice actresses (namely Sally Cahill and Jolene Andersen), I'm going to instead complain that it's possible the voice director didn't have much time or ability to guide Lily Gao into sounding like the best possible Ada within the time frame of the game's development cycle. At this point in the game's lifespan, you can either suck it up, do something about it, complain on the internet about it, or all of the above, but that doesn't change that we have to accept this is the final version of her voice-over that we the audience will have for Ada in RE4R.

The Cons: What this game does differently to a notable degree that affects one or more aspects negatively
- The change in the radio segments: Leon in the OG had moments where he'd answer his radio to either shoot the breeze or update his status with Hunnigan, and later into RE4, do the same but with Ramon Salazar and Osmund Saddler. In RE4R, Leon's signal jam in the Castle segment means he can only talk to Luis or Ada if they end up contacting him, but Salazar and Saddler's radio bits were completely removed, resulting in weaker presences for these two characters outside of what few cutscenes remained. No quip takes away from the B horror movie vibe the original had, but also means that Salazar and Saddler don't get to banter against Leon and show they have something else under their antagonism for the American Agent sent to save the U.S. President's daughter.

Longwinded as this not-quite-a-review is, I've learned of the character limit and can't fit it all in. But while my opinion is that potential buyer should consider getting this game for themselves to see if it is appealing, I do believe your time will not be wasted playing this rehash of a masterpiece of gaming history. But getting it on sale where possible is definitely worth it.

I have been a massive fan of the Resident Evil games since my childhood. Growing up without a capable PC or console at the time, I wasn't able to play these games when they first came out. Now that I finally have a great PC, I am proudly playing through every single RE game. Even with the massive hype surrounding it, this game exceeded every expectation I had. It takes the legendary foundation of the original and elevates it into a modern masterpiece. The tension, the combat mechanics, the updated story, and the gorgeous atmosphere are absolutely flawless. I wholeheartedly recommend this game to every single gamer out there, whether you are a die-hard fan of the series or a complete newcomer. It isn't just a great RE game; it is easily one of the best games ever made. Do not miss out on this!

There are enough positive reviews for this game so i going to keep mine short.
The game is good. This is a pretty faithfull remake of the original game. There are minor differences (character behaviour, some of the events missing, etc.), but the core parts ot the game are the same.
This version fixed the biggest problem i had with the original, the character movement. In the original game the main character can only look, move with his whole body. You can not look with just your "head".

Should you buy the remake? I think it is worth it. If you own the original then wait for a sale, if you do not own the original it could even worth buying it for full price.

Resident Evil 4 (original) was my favorite game in the franchise and of all time for a long time. I was very excited to play this remake, and what I can say is: I wasn't disappointed at all. The remake brought life to the game, transforming Ashley, who was a much-hated and irrelevant character in the story (despite the story being about rescuing her), into an adorable character that you feel a real desire to save. Luis, who was also irrelevant in the original story, also became a more significant and fun character. This pattern continues for all the other characters.
I don't even need to talk about the design of each one; they are all absolutely beautiful and of absurd quality. (Many hate Salazar's new design, but I like it; I think it brought a more sinister air to him. In the original, he looked too young for his age.)
The remake managed to take a classic game remembered with great affection and transform it into something even better, definitely one of the best remakes in the saga and in games in general.
Ada's DLC was even more fun than I remembered it being in the original; it was an incredible experience playing with Ada, who now has much more personality and charm.

And well… everyone already knows that Leon is hot, plus he's a great character to play. I think that's more due to the fluidity of the game's combat (which was a problem in the original) than to Leon himself, but playing with a hot character is always good.

Getting the platinum trophy was a painful but infinitely fun experience, I recommend it!

IMPORTANT: DOES NOT RUN WELL ON CROSSOVER
If you have a Mac, I recommend buying and playing RE games through the app store if possible. I loved this game when playing it native, it's a great game, but it does not run on Crossover on Mac easily with graphics issues that make the game unplayable.
Great game though!

I finished Resident Evil 4 a few months ago, yet only now did I finally sit down to write this review — and honestly, the game still feels fresh in my memory. I won’t pretend to say anything groundbreaking, but for someone like me, whose first real introduction to the series was Resident Evil 2 Remake, this entry felt absolutely cosmic.

The visual overhaul alone deserves praise. Compared to the original, this remake feels like an entirely different experience — modern, atmospheric, and incredibly polished. But what truly hooked me wasn’t just the graphics; it was the story. Surprisingly long, gripping from start to finish, and filled with moments that kept me playing almost without stopping. It delivered emotions I didn’t expect, even though I already knew the franchise’s reputation.

Now I’m just waiting for the chance to dive into Resident Evil: Requiem when it releases.

And of course… Ada Wong. What can I say — she steals every scene she appears in.

A fantastic remake, worth every minute and every dollar.

THE BEST.
Even better than Resident Evil 2, just pure exploration, different chapters, enemies, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ hell love the horror.
Plus its so replayable, masterpiece.
11/10

This is an excellent game; the gameplay is so smooth, satisfying, and addictive; Separate Ways DLC is way better than in the original, and Ashley is better written and designed to be less annoying, while also having the best glow-up in the remake series. 

Two flaws that bothered me the most and prevented me from considering this game a 10/10; something feels off about the atmosphere when compared to the original. I still get chills when I hear the chanting in the original, but in the remake, it feels like a poor imitation; even the merchant no longer sounds mysterious. The second is how bad the voice acting for Ada is; it's so awful that I had to use a mod to make it bearable. Whoever heard that performance and thought it was fine should get fired, especially after the stellar performance we got in RE2R from Jolene Andersen, but for some reason, she was replaced by the actress from the awful movie "Welcome to Raccoon City", a decision that baffles me to this day.

Other than that, nothing else really bothered me that much, except maybe the amount of yellow paint, which is getting comically ridiculous with each game, and having to buy the original version soundtrack instead of including it in the base game.

Overall, despite my criticism, which comes from deep love for the original, I think this is an amazing game and even one of the best and most fun I have had in the last 5 years. It only shows weakness when compared to the original, which is sadly very common with modern remakes, but this is one of the better remakes of the modern era. I highly recommend it while also recommending giving the original a try if you are interested in the genre.

Deadass one of the most fun games ive ever played. Everything about this game is just so perfect and hits every nerve receptor in my brain, but in a way that makes me feel emotion, not just dopamine and endorphins. The characters are awesome, the gameplay is incredibly fun, the story is very intriguing and actually interesting, and the graphics are absolutely beautiful. Also the replay value is insane, i could easily play this game for another 50 hours at least just unlocking all the items and weapons and doing all the challenges and achievments. The amount of content you get for paying $40, or in my case: $15 bc it was on sale is actually insane. And it's not ♥♥♥♥♥♥ lazy content either, it is all quality and actually enjoyable. Buy this game, one of the best ones made.

Coming off from RE2R and RE3R, my hopes for this game were high and it doesn't dissapoint at all. This is Resident Evil at its best, the gameplay loop of shooting, melee, parrying has been perfected to the maximum and it just flows supremely. All characters are awesome, the boss fights are great but the best part of the game is its setting, the village, the castle and the factory all are just *chefs kiss*. The Separate Ways DLC with Ada is equally amazing and I've already spent countless hours in the Mercenaries mode, it's really addictive. This game does not miss and it's a straight up 10/10 for me.
Peak game with peak optimization (except for the parts where the rain comes in but still playable frame drops below 50 to 60 sometimes)

Its honestly one of the best Resident Evils I have played. It leans more into a shooter/survival horror than some of the other titles, and I thought I wouldn't like that. But I ended up enjoying it and got used to it for the most part. I found the story to be really interesting and have really good set pieces. There is a surprising amount of content for a remake. Taking me 27 hours to beat it with doing the merchant quests, treasure hunting, hardcore mode, and the pirate mini game. The only downside that I can think of for this game is the Ashley sections feel obnoxious. Especially on higher difficulties, it is easy for enemies to steal Ashley and get forced into a game over screen. Better yet, you accidently shoot her and its also a forced game over. It just wasn't fun for me. Other than that, I enjoyed it on the same level as Biohazard and Village.
Finally played through RE4, even if it was the remake. Still, well done and fun. If it's under 30 bucks it's a must buy I think.

Honestly one of the most immersive remasters I’ve played. It keeps everything that made the original RE4 iconic while improving the graphics, atmosphere, and gameplay in a way that feels fresh but still nostalgic. Overall, I highly recommend it, especially if you grew up playing the original RE4.

The original was already great, but this remake is amazing. Great gameplay, so much to explore and beautiful graphics.

With what I have on my Playstation, I believe I had put around 500 hours into this game.... worth it. Still love this game.... probably more than the original..... which I have bought 5 times (Gamecube, Switch, Xbox, Steam, and VR).

Capcom took a classic, sanded all of the edges off and gave it a coat of polish.
Having grown up with the original release of Resident Evil 4, I can't review the 2023 remake without bias. The basic bones of the RE4 are here, but everything unique about it has been trimmed away to create a very standardized experience. But I'm not saying this game is bad, it just won't ever have the same impact as the original. In the spirt of generosity here is a short bullet list of things which I like and consider actual improvements (though they don't add to a superior experience):
* Luis has been given a much larger role in the story and his character is more fleshed out. And somehow Capcom managed to keep most of his original charm, despite almost everybody else having gotten a personality deletion.
* Being able to quick swap your weapons without accessing the inventory is just great. You can freely customize the hotkeys to keep your favorite weapons ready to go
* Free movement of your character (though this came at the cost of the original's very addictive "stop and pop" gameplay loop)
* revised treasure and gem slotting system
* More side quests, including minibosses and a vastly improved shooting gallery minigame
* high fidelity graphics and sound (but this comes at the cost of the original's very stylish character designs and soundtrack)
finally i can play my whitelist game!! even thought i never play the original cause skill issue:D, but anyway love this gamee! all the series resident evil!

My absolute favorite of the entire series! Ridiculous plot (as intended) but such a fun thriller. Things move along rather than getting bogged down in back and forth traversal as in the other games. Breathless pace, I loved it!

I liked RE4R but for some reason I felt slightly disappointed by the end of the game. 

I feel like a lot of aspects of the game have little context or explanation and are just thrown in the game because (the Verdugo, Mendez, Salazar, & Merchant). I'm aware the Verdugo appears in Ada's playthrough but that's DLC content and an additional $10 on top of a $40 game. I wish they would've just did what they did with RE2R and made both playable in the same game not a DLC, feels cash grabby. 

The merchant was very jarring to me having come from RE2R & RE3R, its just random as hell and sort of detracts from the "survival" aspect of the game when you can just buy stuff. Mendez was in the game for a total of like 15 minutes, was easy to defeat, and frankly forgettable. I think the village was great the castle sequence started to go in the right direction but the Island is where it all goes off the rails and becomes a call of duty/tomb raider reminiscent sequence in my opinion. 

Overall the game is good fun and I appreciate the length coming from RE3R which was painfully short. It does feel a bit like RE4R is trying to be too many games in one and it does not at any point feel "scary", sense of urgency or dread like RE2R and even RE3R did. I know people see this remake as the "best" of all remakes but I would beg to argue that RE2R takes the cake for best remake that better represents the soul of Resident Evil games.
One of, if not arguably, the best Resident Evil game. The original and the remake are perfect, chefs kiss. Also leon...
